coredata.dc:title: Yeast Cryptococcus Podzolicus derived fluorescent carbon dots for multicolour cellular imaging and high selectivity detection of pollutant
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Xiuling
Yuan, Xiaotian
Nian, Hongjuan
Song, Pengfei
Xiang, Yingying
Wei, Yunlin
Wang, Suping
Qin, Kunhao
Zhang, Qi
Tu, Yujiao
coredata.dc:description: Novel blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs-CP) deriving from yeast Cryptococcus podzolicus 5–2 were successfully prepared via a facile and green hydrothermal synthesis method. It exhibited uniform particle size and possessed good excitation-dependent characteristic. The dead bacterial cells were selectively labled by the CDs-CP, but the live ones not, indicating good potential for the live/dead bacterial differentiation. It was found that the prepared CDs-CP showed low biotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility with multicolour fluorescence emission properties, which provided a new insight on the bioimaging application. In addition, the prepared CDs-CP exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity in Ag(I), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) detection with excellent linear ranges of 0–15 μM (R2 = 0.9945), 0–30 μM (R2 = 0.9959) and 0–37.5 μM (R2 = 0.9982), respectively. The low detection limits of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP were 113.57 nM, 73.36 nM and 86.47 nM, respectively. It revealed that static quenching mechanism dominated in Ag(I) quenching of the CDs-CP fluorescent, while those of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP were inner filter effect. The fluorescent CDs-CP was successfully applied to determine the Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real water samples with satisfactory results. Therefore, it is a good candidate for bioimaging application and environmental pollutant detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cryptococcus podzolicus
Bioimaging
Live/dead bacterial differentiation
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching test of Ag(I), 4-NP and 2,4-DNP
Selectivity of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4- NP detection
Possible mechanism of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (inset: HR-TEM) and (b) FTIR spectrum and (c) XRD pattern of the Cryptococcus Podzolicus-derived CDs-CP.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the prepared CDs-CP. High-resolution C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c), and O1s spectra (d).
(a) UV–Vis, excitation and emission spectra of CDs-CP, (b) Emission of CDs-CP at various excitation wavelengths.
Live and dead bacteria labeled with prepared CDs-CP at bright field and fluorescence images by CLSM.
CLSM images of HeLa and RD cells with CDs-CP.
Plant leaf imaging with injection of CDs-CP.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of metal ions (a) and aromatic compounds (b) to the CDs-CP.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs-CP with various concentrations of Ag(I) (0–150 μM) (a), 2,4-DNP (0–100 μM) (b) and 4-NP (0–100 μM) (c). Relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0−F)/F0 and Ag(I) (0–150 μM) (d), 2,4-DNP (0–100 μM) (e) and 4-NP (0–100 μM) (f) concentration. Linear relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and Ag(I) (0–15 μM) (g), 2,4-DNP (0–30 μM) (h) and 4-NP (0–37.5 μM) (i) concentration.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs-CP, Ag(I), and CDs-CP + Ag(I)(a), CDs-CP, 2,4-DNP, and CDs-CP+2,4-DNP (b), CDs-CP, 4-NP, and CDs-CP+4-NP (c); the FL decay lifetime of CDs-CP and CDs-CP + Ag(I) (d), CDs-CP and CDs-CP+2,4-DNP (e), CDs-CP and CDs-CP+4-NP (f); UV–Vis absorption of Ag(I) and fluorescence spectra of CDs-CP (g), UV–Vis absorption of 2,4-DNP and fluorescence spectra of CDs-CP (h), UV–Vis absorption of 4-NP and fluorescence spectra of CDs-CP (i).
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of Ag(I) determination.
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of 4-NP determination.
Analytical results of 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) detection in the water samples with CDs-CP (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Yeast Cryptococcus Podzolicus derived fluorescent carbon dots for multicolour cellular imaging and high selectivity detection of pollutant

Yunnan Key Laboratory of Food-Safety Testing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Science and Technology, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs-CP) deriving from yeast Cryptococcus podzolicus 5–2 were successfully prepared via a facile and green hydrothermal synthesis method. It exhibited uniform particle size and possessed good excitation-dependent characteristic. The dead bacterial cells were selectively labled by the CDs-CP, but the live ones not, indicating good potential for the live/dead bacterial differentiation. It was found that the prepared CDs-CP showed low biotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility with multicolour fluorescence emission properties, which provided a new insight on the bioimaging application. In addition, the prepared CDs-CP exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity in Ag(I), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) detection with excellent linear ranges of 0–15 μM (R2 = 0.9945), 0–30 μM (R2 = 0.9959) and 0–37.5 μM (R2 = 0.9982), respectively. The low detection limits of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP were 113.57 nM, 73.36 nM and 86.47 nM, respectively. It revealed that static quenching mechanism dominated in Ag(I) quenching of the CDs-CP fluorescent, while those of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP were inner filter effect. The fluorescent CDs-CP was successfully applied to determine the Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real water samples with satisfactory results. Therefore, it is a good candidate for bioimaging application and environmental pollutant detection.

Schematic of the synthetic route for fluorescent CDs-CP and the applications in the live/dead bacterial differentiation, cell and plant leaf bioimaging, and detection for Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of bacterial viability is very important to detect pathogen in food, water body, hospital and clinical samples. Fluorescence staining is normally used to evaluate bacterial vitality [1]. As traditional commercial fluorescent dyes, propidium iodide and propidium azide, which can penetrate the cell wall of dead bacteria, are commonly used in DNA dying. However, these commercial dyes are usually expensive, toxic and experience photobleaching. So, it is urgent to develop dyes with low toxicity, water solubility and low cost to monitor the viability of bacteria.

With the continuous advancement of mankind and the rapid development of industrial technology, many artificial chemicals and by-products have been produced. The continuous release of these harmful pollutant not only affects the quality of the environment, but also poses a threat to human health [2]. Environmental pollution is a major issue, especially organic pollution and heavy metal pollution.

Phenolic wastewater is one of the most dangerous kinds of industrial wastewater in the world and is a major source of water pollution in the environment. Phenol is contained in wastewater discharged from many industrial fields such as metallurgy, coking, refining and petrochemical and chemical organic synthesis [3–5]. p-nitrophenol (4-NP) is one of the most toxic pollutants derived from the manufacture of pesticide, medicine, dye, leather anti-fungal agent and other industrial products [6]. 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) is used as a preservative and as a non-selective bioaccumulation pesticide. 2,4-DNP is an important chemical intermediate for sulphur dyes, wood preservatives and picric acids [7–9]. It can be released to the atmosphere along with motor vehicle exhaust, industrial combustion waste, and nitrogen and other chemicals in the air. Thus, 4-NP and 2,4-DNP have been enlisted in the “Priority Pollutant List” by U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) due to their toxicity and carcinogenicity [10].

Heavy metal is the main pollutant in environment. Ag(I) is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions. Due to wide employment in industry, they could pose serious threats to environment and living organisms [11,12]. Due to binding to various metabolites and inactivate sulfhydryl enzymes, Ag(I) can lead to significant cytopathogenic effects on different kinds of cell types [13,14]. Moreover, there is no silver poisoning and effective antidote. Therefore, it is highly desired to develop an accurate monitoring system.

Due to their easy preparation, high fluorescence intensity, low toxicity and good biocompatibility properties [15–19], carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more attention and been applied in bioimaging [20–23], detecting metal ions, such as Hg(II) [24], Ag(I) [25], Fe(III) [23,26], Cr(VI) [27], detecting 4-NP [28] and drug delivery [29].

At present, there are many reports on the preparation of CDs from biomass [24,25], but relatively few deriving from microbial substrates, which are mainly from bacteria [1,30], and even fewer on those from yeast [31]. Yeast, a single-cell fungus ubiquitous in nature, is a typical heterotrophic facultative anaerobic microbe that can survive in both aerobic and anaerobic conditions. Cryptococcus podzolicus 5–2 (GenBank No. KJ701018), which was isolated from soil sample of tea plantation in Yunnan Province, China, and showed good Al(ll) tolerance ability. C. podzolicus is a member of the Basidiomycota, Tremellomycetes, Tremellales, Tremellaceae. Since C. podzolicus contains C, N, O, S and P, it can be used for doping in CDs without addition of any other additive. Herein, we prepare blue fluorescent CDs with cheap and green non-toxic fungus biomass raw materials (i.e. C. podzolicus 5–2) in the water solvent through a one-step hydrothermal method. In this paper, the C. podzolicus 5-2 derived CDs (CDs-CP) was employed for biosensing applications, and the preparation, properties and applications of CDs-CP are systematically studied. The prepared CDs-CP perform a multifunctional role in live/dead bacterial differentiation, bioimaging and detection of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in a selective way with a high selectivity. To our knowledge, this is the first report on CDs-CP deriving from C. podzolicus 5–2 for live/dead bacterial differentiation, bioimaging and environmental pollutant detection.

All analytical reagents, including phenol (P), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), nonylphenol (NP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), m-aminophenol (MAP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), p-aminophenol (PAP), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), o-aminophenol (OAP), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (2,4,6-TCP), Ni(NO3)2, HgCl2, AlCl3, CuSO4, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, CrCl3, MnSO4, CoCl2, CrCl6, MgCl2, BaCl2, CsCl and FeCl3, were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid media, including 10 g/L peptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, and 10 g/L NaCl (pH 7.0), were used to incubate Escherichia coli BW25113, Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus cereus MYB41-22. Brain Heart Infusion (BHI) culture medium, purchased from Oxoid, Ltd., were used to incubate E. faecalis YN771 (GenBank No. MH919370). Yeast extract Peptone Dextrose (YPD) medium, including 20 g/L peptone, 10 g/L yeast extract, and 20 g/L glucose (pH 5.4), were used to incubate yeast C. podzolicus 5–2 (GenBank No. KJ701018).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, Netherlands) was used to obtain TEM images of CDs-CP. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany) was employed to determine the surface chemistry characteristics of CDs-CP. XPS spectra (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.). The crystalline phases were measured by XRD on a Bruker D8 Advance. Fluorescence emission spectrum (G9800A fluorospectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies, USA) and UV–Vis absorption spectrum (UV-2600 spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan) were used to determine the optical properties of CDs-CP. Spectrofluorometer (FS5, England) was used to determinate the lifetime. A vacuum freeze-drying system (CHRIST ALPHA 1–4, Germany) was employed to dry the nanomaterials. The zeta average potential was determined by Zeta potential analyser (Microtrac Inc.). IVIS Lumina Series III (PerkinElmer Health Sciences, Inc.) was used for leaf imaging.

The CDs-CP derived from C. podzolicus 5–2 were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. The culture of C. podzolicus 5–2 was incubated in YPD medium for 24 h at 28 °C with 150 rpm, and then collected and centrifuged at 13,000 g for 10 min (Beckman Avanti J-25, CA, USA). The pellets were dissolved in 30 mL sterile water, and then transferred to 50 mL high-pressure autoclave for reaction at 200 °C for 6 h. The product was cooled naturally, centrifuged (13,000 g, 30 min), filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and then stored (4 °C).

Four kinds of bacteria were used as the objective of differentiation with prepared CDs-CP, i.e., E. coli BW25113 (Gram-negative bacterium), E. faecalis YN771 (Gram-negative bacterium), B. cereus 41–22 (Gram-positive bacterium) and S. aureus (Gram-positive bacterium). E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41–22 and S. aureus were incubated in LB liquid media (37 °C, 12 h, 150 rpm), while E. faecalis YN771 in BHI liquid media (28 °C, 12 h, 150 rpm). After centrifugation (10,000 g, 2 min) the supernatant was discarded. The precipitate is washed three times with sterile water. Dead bacterial cells were obtained by treatment with 4% formaldehyde solution for 2 h. Four kinds of live or dead bacterial cells (109 cfu/mL) were mixed with 500 μg/mL CDs-CP (28 °C or 37 °C, 2 h, 150 rpm). After centrifugation (10,000 g, 2 min) the supernatant was discarded. The precipitate is washed three times with sterile water to remove the excess CDs-CP. Then all bacterial cells were suspended with 500 μL sterile water and imaged under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) through excitation at 405 nm, 488 nm and 543 nm. Meanwhile, the zeta average potentials of live/dead bacterial samples with or without CDs-CP were measured.

Human malignant embryonal rhabdomyoma (RD) cells and HeLa cells were cultured with 10% fetal bovine serum DMEM medium and 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Sterilized cell slide was put into a 24-well plate, and then HeLa and RD cells were cultured (5% CO2, 37 °C, 24 h) with different concentration CDs-CP (100, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 μg/mL)[15,25]. Then 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT) method was employed to evaluate the viability of RD and HeLa cells.

The RD and HeLa cells were serum-starved for 1 h after 24 h cultivation, and the cells were washed with phosphate buffer solution after the medium was removed. Then 100 μg/mL CDs-CP were added and incubated (37 °C, 2 h). All samples were fixed 30 min with 4% paraformaldehyde, and then cell imaging was performed on CLSM [15,25].

To further investigate the biodistribution imaging of the prepared CDs-CP in plant, leaves of Pharbitis purpurea (L.) Voisgt and Nicandra physaloides, with around 3–5 cm length, were collected and surfaces sterilized. The 100 μg/mL CDs-CP were injected into the base of the sterilized P. purpurea (L.) Voisgt leaves, while 100 μg/mL CDs-CP were injected from three points into the middle of the sterilized Nicandra physaloides leaves. The leaves were observed under the IVIS Lumina Series III imaging system (exposure time 0.2 s) at regular intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching test of Ag(I), 4-NP and 2,4-DNP
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2 μL of prepared CDs-CP solution and 0.998 mL phosphate buffer solution were mixed, added with different concentrations Ag(I)/2,4-DNP/4-NP solution, and then diluted to 4.0 mL with deionized water. The PL intensity was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer under the optimal excitation wavelength (350 nm) at the slit width of 5.0 nm. The selectivity of Ag(I)/2,4-DNP/4-NP and other substances (1 mM concentration) was investigated in order to evaluate the coexisting interfering substances on the PL intensity. These substances included P, 4-CP, NP, PAP, 4-NP, 2,4-DCP, OAP, 2,4-DNP, MAP, 2,4,6-TCP, HgCl2, CuSO4, CrCl6, CoCl2, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, CrCl3, AlCl3, MnSO4, FeCl3, MgCl2, BaCl2, Ni(NO3)2 and CsCl.

The quantum yield (QY) was determined according to the comparative method. The QY of quinine sulfate dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1 M) was 54% at excited wavelength 360 nm, and assigned as a reference. [32] The following equation was applied:
Q
CDs = Q
R (I
CDs /I
R ) (A
R /A
CDs ) (η
CDs
2/η
R
2)
Where the subscript CDs refers to prepared CDs-CP and R refers to reference; Q
CDs refers to QY of the prepared fluorescence CDs-CP, I is the integrated emission intensity, η is the solvent refractive index (η
R = 1.33), and A refers to the absorbance value at the excitation wavelength.

The fluorescence lifetime of CDs-CP was calculated on a spectrometer equipped with an LED laser under excitation wavelength of 380 nm at room temperature less than 20 °C. Firstly, the absorbance of CDs-CP solution was determined to be less than 0.1 by ultraviolet spectrophotometer under the optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Then, the fluorescence attenuation curve was obtained based on time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique.

Average lifetime model for total lifetime calculation of time-resolved fluorescence spectra is as follows:
τ =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n
( n = 1,2,3…… )
Where A
n refers to the pre-exponential factor of time resolved decay lifetime, τ
n refers to time resolved decay lifetime, τ refers to the average fluorescence lifetime.

The fitting model of time-resolved fluorescence spectra is an E-exponential model:
Y= B + A1*exp(-t/τ1) + … + An*exp(-t/τn) (n = 1,2,3……)
Where τ
n refers to time resolved decay lifetime, A
n refers to the pre-exponential factor of time resolved decay lifetime.

To investigate the practicality of the prepared fluorescence CDs-CP sensor system, standard addition recovery experiment was performed in tap, lake and waste water with and without 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I), respectively. The water samples were collected and centrifuged (13,000 g, 15 min) and then filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove impurities. The 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) content in the sample was detected by CDs-CP sensor system, and then the standard addition recovery method was performed. Different spiked 5 μg/mL 2,4-DNP, 10 μg/mL 4-NP and 15 μg/mL Ag(I) were added into the samples, then 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) content was detected by CDs-CP sensor system. The experiment was performed in triplicate. The percentage recoveries and relative standard deviation (%RSD) were obtained.

TEM was employed to investigate the morphological characteristics and particle size distribution of CDs-CP (Fig. 1 a). It was found that the CDs-CP have uniform spherical particles (2.2–8.5 nm) with the average diameter about 4.8 nm. FTIR was used to determine the surface chemistry characteristics of the prepared CDs-CP (Fig. 1b). A strong absorption peak at 3448 cm−1 displays the presence of N-H or O-H bonds [15]; the peak at 1664 cm−1 denotes the C=C or N-H bonds; the peak at 1403 cm−1 indicates C-H, C-N or O=C-H bonds; and the peak at 1080 cm−1 reveals C-O-C or C-N bonds [33]. The XRD pattern exhibits good crystallinity with a broad peak at 2θ = 21.09°, indicating the little crystalline nature of CDs-CP (Fig. 1c).

The five peaks at 285.14 eV, 531.79 eV, 393.84 eV, 163.73 eV and 133.75 eV appear in the full scan XPS spectra, attributing to C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p and P2p respectively (Fig. 2 a). The atom ratios of these elements are 70.09%, 19.68%, 9.32%, 0.53% and 0.37% respectively. The peaks from C1s spectrum at 284.29 eV, 284.92 eV, 286.31 eV and 287.92 eV are attributed to C=C, C-C, C-N or C-O and C=O [34], respectively (Fig. 2b). The two peaks appearing at 399.3 eV and 400.4 eV in N1s spectrum are attributed to N-H and N-C [34], respectively (Fig. 2c); the two peaks from O1s spectrum at 531.4 eV and 532.2 eV are attributed to C=O and C-O (Fig. 2d) [34]. The result of the XPS diagram is consistent with that of the FTIR spectrum, indicating that the prepared CDs-CP maybe have abundant hydrophilic and amino functional groups on the surface, and possess good water solubility [28].

The optical properties of the prepared CDs-CP were determined by fluorescence emission spectrum and UV–Vis absorption spectrum [15]. CDs-CP liquid is clear and colorless under sunlight while blue after UV excitation, indicating that the fluorescent CDs-CP have been successfully prepared (Fig. 3 a, inset). It shows that a weak absorption peak around 260 nm is attributed to the π-π* transitions (C=C bond) (Fig. 3a) [15,28,35]. The maximum emission wavelength is 440 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength (350 nm) (Fig. 3b). When the excitation wavelength shifts from 280 to 340 nm, the PL intensity increases and when it shifts from 350 to 400 nm, the PL intensity reduces; at the same time, the emission peak shows a slight red-shift, indicating good excitation-dependent feature of the prepared CDs-CP (Fig. 3b).

The fluorescence stability of the CDs-CP was determined under different salt concentrations and pH values. It was found that the PL intensity only fluctuates slightly when the NaCl solution concentration increases (0–1.0 mM) (Fig. S1a), indicating good fluorescence stability [15]. With the pH buffer changing from 2.2 to 12.0, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-CP increases from pH 2.2 to 6.0, and then decreases from pH 6.0 to 12.0, which demonstrates that the prepared CDs-CP was stable in pH 3.0–9.0 (Fig. S1b).

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs-CP was determined to be 3.99% (I
CDs = 219.976 a.u., I
R = 671.536 a.u., A
R = 0.014, A
CDs = 0.062, η
R = 1.33) based on standard quinine sulfate (QY = 54%).

Average lifetime of CDs-CP was determined to be 3.01 ns by double exponential fitting (A
1 = 8342.26637, τ
1 = 6.5112 ns, A
2 = 83941.92863, τ
2 = 1.60582 ns) at Ex = 350.

The prepared CDs-CP were designed for bacterial imaging. The live bacteria (E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41–22, S. aureus and E. faecalis YN771) could not be labeled by the fluorescent multicolour probe CDs-CP (Fig. 4 ). Then the possibility of labelling dead bacteria was investigated. Dead bacterial cells were treated with 2% formaldehyde solution for 2 h. It indicated that all dead bacterial cells could be distinctly labeled by CDs-CP (100 μg/mL) and observed by CLSM in blue, green and red colours (Fig. 4). It indicats that the CDs-CP is a good candidate for the live/dead bacterial differentiation and bioimaging [1].

It shows that the prepared CDs-CP can successfully discriminate live/dead bacteria with excellent selectivity. It has been reported that electrostatic interaction between CDs-CP and bacteria and the selective permeability of bacteria towards CDs-CP maybe the main determinants of live/dead cell differentiation [1]. The average value of zeta potential of CDs-CP in water is measured to be −30.25 mV, which accords with the negatively charged nature of C. podzolicus 5–2 cells (−36.4 mV), revealing the presence of negatively charged groups on the prepared CDs-CP surfaces, such as carboxyl groups [24]. The zeta potential values of live E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41–22, S. aureus and E. faecalis YN771 are measured to be −43.63 mV, −40.00 mV, −30.50 mV and −38.30 mV, respectively. The zeta potential values of live E. coli BW25113 with CDs-CP, B. cereus 41–22 with CDs-CP, S. aureus with CDs-CP and E. faecalis YN771 with CDs-CP are measured to be −40.01 mV, −36.14 mV, −26.86 mV and −35.12 mV, respectively. Due to the stronger electrostatic repulsion between live bacteria and CDs-CP, live bacterial cells cannot be labeled by CDs-CP (Fig. 4). After treatment with 4% formaldehyde, the zeta potential values of dead E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41–22, S. aureus and E. faecalis YN771 are measured to be −23.07 mV, −22.15 mV, −19.03 mV and −22.67 mV, respectively, while the zeta potential values of dead E. coli BW25113 with CDs-CP, B. cereus 41–22 with CDs-CP, S. aureus with CDs-CP and E. faecalis YN771 with CDs-CP are measured to be −18.61 mV, −18.03 mV, −15.94 mV and −17.87 mV, respectively. Indicating that electrostatic interaction between CDs-CP surface and dead bacteria is greatly weakened. Therefore, dead bacterial cells can be labeled by CDs-CP. It's worth noting that there is uncertainty of all the measurements of the zeta potential. Zeta potential is related to pH, particle concentration, solvent and temperature. The experiment was performed in triplicate.

It is well known that bacterium cell membranes have selective permeability, and small molecular substances entering cells usually require transportation by ion channels or transmembrane proteins, so live bacteria cells cannot be labeled. However, the cell walls or membranes of dead bacteria have been destroyed and selective permeability deduced, so CDs-CP can successfully label the dead bacterial cells. Hua et al. have reported the live/dead bacterial differentiation with CDs derived from S. aureus and E. coli [1], but there is no report on live/dead bacterial differentiation with CDs derived from C. podzolicus. Therefore, CDs-CP have excellent application prospects in environmental monitoring, medical and health care, food processing and pharmaceutical industry [1].

In order to evaluate the bioimaging application potential, the cytotoxicity of the prepared CDs-CP was assayed by the way of MTT. The viability of the HeLa and RD cells cultivated with CDs-CP was measured with untreated cells as control. It was found that the viability of cells showed no significant decrease even when the CDs-CP concentration reached 1000 μg/mL (Fig. S2), indicating low cytotoxicity of the prepared CDs-CP.

Nanomaterials has been used in medical diagnosis, and biocompatibility is one of the important determinants of its application. The multicolour PL and good biocompatibility demonstrated that the prepared CDs-CP were suitable for bioimaging applications, and then in vitro cell imaging was carried out by CLSM. After incubation with 100 μg/mL CDs-CP at 37 °C for 2 h, the HeLa and RD cells emitted different fluorescence in the cell nuclei and were obviously labeled by CDs-CP. All green, blue and red channels were clearly observed (Fig. 5 ). The CLSM results clearly showed the optical bioimaging potential of CDs-CP at the cellular level. Therefore, CDs-CP can be used as a good candidate dye for cell nuclear labeling. Furthermore, we did not observe the substantial morphological changes in HeLa and RD cells by treating with CDs-CP, which further verified the low cytotoxicity of CDs-CP.

Why the CDs are internalized in live HeLa and RD cells while this is not possible in the previous bacteria samples? We think maybe structural difference between cells and bacteria plays a key role. Bacteria have cell walls structure, whereas HeLa and RD cells do not. Live bacteria have intact cell walls that make it difficult for CDs-CP to enter the bacteria cell. For HeLa and RD cells, cell membrane regulates the flow of substances, then CDs-CP was delivered into the cell by some surface proteins, and bind to DNA, so HeLa and RD cells could be labeled by CDs-CP, whereas living bacteria cannot be labeled by CDs-CP.

The biodistribution of CDs-CP in plant leaf was performed to verify the plant imaging. The fluorescence intensity increased over time (Fig. 6 ), indicating that CDs-CP successfully entered the leaves system of Pharbitis purpurea (L.) Voisgt and Nicandra physaloides, and the fluorescence generation sites were spread throughout the leaves cells rather than attached to the injection sites of leaves. Therefore, CDs-CP was distributed in the leaves body via leaf vein and transported in the leaf. The plant leaf bioimaging successfully broadens the application of carbon dots in bioimaging, and provides a new perspective for plant marker research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4- NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the prepared CDs-CP was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of different possible interfering substances was monitored. We found that 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) could effectively quench the PL intensity of CDs-CP (Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7b). So, high selectivity between CDs-CP and Ag(I) or 2,4-DNP and 4-NP is presented. It indicates that CDs-CP can be used to detect Ag(I), 4-NP and 2,4-DNP.

The pH affects not only the CDs PL intensity, but also the quenching efficiency of quenchers on CD. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-CP is related to pH and NaCl concentration (0–1 M) (Figs. S2a and S2b). Within the pH 2.2–9.0 for Ag(I) detection, the PL intensity increases and the maximum quenching efficiency is obtained, but after pH 9.0, the PL intensity decreases. It shows that the fluorescence quenching reaction reaches the largest at pH 9.0, so pH 9.0 is the optimum pH value for Ag(I) detection (Fig. S3a). Within the pH 2.2–6.0 for 2,4-DNP detection, the PL intensity increases and the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency is reached, but after pH 6.0, the PL intensity decreases. It shows that the optimum pH value of 2,4-DNP detection is pH 6.0 (Fig. S3b). Within the pH 2.2–7.0 for 4-NP detection, the PL intensity increases and the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency is reached, but after pH 7.0, the PL intensity decreases [12]. It shows that the optimum pH value for 4-NP detection is pH 7.0 (Fig. S3c). In order to investigate the interference with each other of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP towards CDs-CP were performed at pH 6.0, pH 7.0 and pH 9.0. (F0-F)/F0 refers to the fluorescence quenching efficiency, F0 is the PL intensity of CDs-CP, F is the quenched fluorescence intensity of the CDs-CP after addition of the quenchers. It showed that the three types of test objects did not interfere with each other at different pHs (Fig. S4).

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the relation between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 of CDs-CP and the concentration of Ag(I) or 2,4-DNP or 4-NP was studied. As shown in Fig. 8 a -8c, the PL intensity of CDs-CP decreases with the increase of Ag(I) or 2,4-DNP or 4-NP concentration (Fig. 8d–f). The ratio of PL intensity is linear with the concentrations of Ag(I) (0–15 μM, R2 = 0.9945) (Figs. 8g), 2,4-DNP (0–30 μM, R2 = 0.9959) (Fig. 8h) and 4-NP (0–37.5 μM, R2 = 0.9982) (Fig. 8i). Correlation coefficients (R2) are 0.9945, 0.9959 and 0.9982, respectively, meanwhile slopes are 0.01623, 0.01803, 0.01017, and intercepts are 0.01741, 0.00739 and −0.0016, respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP are 113.57 nM, 73.36 nM and 86.47 nM based on 3σ/slope (where σ is the standard deviation), respectively (Table 1 and Table 2 ), and the S/N (signal-to-noise) is 3. S/N ratio is a measure of how the signal from the defect compares to other background reflections (categorized as “noise”). S/N = H/h, H is the signal power, h is noise power. The limit of quantification (LOQ) of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP are 378.57 nM, 244.53 nM and 288.23 nM based on 10σ/slope (where σ is the standard deviation), respectively, and the S/N ratio is 10. The pollution of 2,4-DNP in environment is serious, so current research on 2,4-DNP mainly focuses on the adsorption and removal of 2,4-DNP in contaminated samples [2,36]. There are relatively few reports on 2,4-DNP detection by CDs, so the analyses of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP are of important practical significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanisms of quenchers to fluorescence CDs mainly include static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [6,45].

To verify the quenching mechanisms of 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) towards CDs-CP, UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs-CP, 2,4-DNP, 4- NP, Ag(I), and CDs-CP with 2,4-DNP, 4-NP or Ag(I) were determined (Fig. 9 a–c). We can find that the absorption of the CDs-CP changes with or without Ag(l), which indicates that new substance has been formed (Fig. 9a), therefore, the quenching process maybe SQ. The absorption of the CDs-CP does not change with or without 2,4-DNP (Fig. 9b) and 4-NP (Fig. 9c), which indicates that no new substance is formed, thus, the possibility of SQ process was eliminated.

As shown in Fig. 9d–f, the FL decay lifetime does not change without (3.01 ns) or with Ag(1) (2.97 ns), 2,4-DNP (2.98 ns) and 4-NP (3.00 ns) (Fig. 9d–f). So the quenching mechanism of Ag(I) may be SQ, while that of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP should be IFE [46].

The absorption spectrum of Ag(I) does not overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of the prepared CDs-CP (Fig. 9g), eliminating the possibility of FRET and IFE in the quenching process of Ag(I) towards CDs-CP [47]. The absorption spectrum of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP overlaps with the excitation spectrum of CDs-CP (Fig. 9h and i). Thus, we infer maybe IFE dominates in quenching process. Usually, when the UV absorption spectrum of the target overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the CDs-CP, IFE works [6,48]. It leads to an apparent decrease of intensity, so actually it is not a quenching process but attributes to the excitation beam attenuation or reabsorption of emitted radiation by the quencher or excessive CDs-CP [37].

Furthermore, the Parker equation was employed to verify the IFE process [45]:
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m

F
cor and F
obsd are the observed PL intensity and the corrected PL intensity, respectively; A
ex and A
em are the absorbance value at the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength, respectively; d refers to the cuvette width; s is the excitation beam thickness; g refers to the distance between the edge of cuvette and excitation beam [45]. With 2,4-DNP concentration increasing (0–25 μM), F
cor
/F
obsd value increases from 1.32 to 2.18, which meets the Parker equation [42]. With 4-NP concentration increasing (0–25 μM), F
cor
/F
obsd value increases from 1.32 to 1.70, which also matches with the Parker equation [42]. Therefore, IFE is further considered to dominate in quenching process of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP towards the fluorescent of CDs-CP.

Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to further investigate the kinetics of SQ mechanism [45].
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]

F
0 is the PL intensity of the prepared CDs-CP, and F is the PL intensity of Ag(I); Kq refers to the quencher coefficient (M−1‧S−1); [Q] refers to the Ag(I) concentration (μM); τ
0 refers to the FL decay lifetime of the prepared CDs-CP (ns), τ
0 = 3.01 ns [45]. A rise of temperature (288.25 K, 298.25 K, 308.25 K) leads to the decrease of slope (0.04938, 0.04838, 0.04045) [42] and Kq (1.641 × 1013 M−1‧S−1, 1.607 × 1013 M−1‧S−1, 1.344 × 1013 M−1‧S−1) (Fig. S5). Kq value obtained in this study is higher than 2 × 1010 M−1‧S−1 (maximum value for scatter characterized by determining the values of Kq and the thermodynamic parameters by varying the collision quenching constant), so the quenching mechanism of Ag(I) belongs to SQ. The affinity constants of CDs-CP for Ag(I) can be deduced from the Stern-Volmer plot, by mean of KSV, which equals: K
SV = kq τ
0 = slope, so the K
SV values of CDs-CP for Ag(I) at 288.25 K, 298.25 K, 308.25 K were 4.938 × 104 M−1, 4.838 × 104 M−1 and 4.045 × 104 M−1, respectively. Extensive studies on quenching mechanisms of Ag(I) on CDs fluorescence sensing of Ag(I) have been reported, most of which are SQ (12, 37–40), which is consistent with the result in our work.

The fluorescent nanoprobe CDs-CP was used to detect Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real tap water, river water and waste water samples. The recovery experiments were carried out by the standard addition method. The good percentage recoveries and %RSD were obtained through analysis. The recoveries of 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) were estimated to fall within 94–105.02%, 93.60–105.66% and 97.33–99.67%, respectively (Table 3 ), and the RSD of 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) were estimated to fall within 2.10–3.67%, 4.05–5.62% and 2.90–4.21%, respectively (Table 3), presenting the sensibility of CDs-CP on 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) in tap water, river water and waste water (n = 3). Therefore, it is easy, sensitive and fast to detect 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) in real water.

A facile and green synthesis for the generation of CDs-CP derived from yeast C. podzolicus was reported with a wide range of applications. Morphology, chemical element composition and optical properties of the prepared CDs-CP were characterized by TEM, XPS, FTIR and UV-Vis. The CDs-CP have uniform spherical particles with the average diameter about 4.8 nm. The XRD pattern exhibits good crystallinity with a broad peak at 2θ = 21.09°, indicating the little crystalline nature of CDs-CP. It was found that CDs-CP possessed abundant hydrophilic, amino functional groups on the surface, good water solubility and excellent fluorescence features. It displayed a strong blue fluorescence emission at 440 nm under 350 nm excitation. The prepared CDs-CP could selectively differentiate live/dead bacterial cells. It showed the low biotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and multicolour fluorescence emission properties of CDs-CP by cell and plant leaf imaging, so it is a good candidate for bioimaging application. Furthermore, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP and Ag(I) could quench the PL intensity of the prepared CDs-CP. Excellent linear relationships were obtained between the fluorescence reaction of CDs-CP and the concentrations of Ag(I), 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in the range 0–15 μM, 0–30 μM and 0–37.5 μM with LOD of 113.57 nM, 73.36 nM and 86.47 nM, respectively. It was revealed that static quenching mechanism dominated in quenching the CDs-CP fluorescent with Ag(I), while the IFE quenching mechanism dominated in 2,4-DNP and 4-NP detection. Thus, as the synthetic method is simple and inexpensive, it is an excellent candidate for bioimaging application and high sensitivity detection of environmental pollutant.

Xiuling Ji: Writing - review & editing. Xiaotian Yuan: Data curation. Hongjuan Nian: Methodology. Pengfei Song: Investigation, Validation. Yingying Xiang: Data curation. Yunlin Wei: Writing - review & editing. Suping Wang: Methodology. Kunhao Qin: Investigation, Validation. Qi Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Yujiao Tu: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially funded by Young Talents Support Program of Yunnan Province (Ten Thousand People Plan) and Science Research Foundation of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education of China (2020J0509).
